system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 2: Jazz Standard Study #1: Autumn Leaves

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 2 — Jazz Standard Study #1: Autumn Leaves

_Pages 20-38._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## AABA form definition

> The song has an AABA form, meaning the first theme (or section) is repeated,
> followed by the second theme, and ending on the original theme.

**Significance (p. 21).** Defines the formal structure of Autumn Leaves, which is essential for understanding how the chord analysis sections map to the song's layout.

## Concert keys and transposing

> Whenever | talk about “common keys” throughout this
> book, | am referring to the concert keys that jazz musicians most
> commonly play these jazz standards in. These are not necessarily
> the original keys, but the keys you will need to know these songs in
> if you want to play them with other musicians

**Significance (p. 22).** Establishes the book's convention for key references, which governs how all subsequent harmonic analysis and transposition instructions must be interpreted.

## Major Diatonic Series of 7th Chords

> This chart is called the Major Diatonic Series of 7* Chords. Essen-
> tially, it shows you how chords can be assigned to the different scale
> degrees of a Major scale.
>
> ¥ The top line shows the note names spelling out a C major scale.
>
> Vv The second line shows the quality of chord created when a 7*
> chord is harmonized with each scale tone.
>
> VY The third line shows the entire 7** chord name.
>
> ¥ The fourth line shows the roman numerals for each scale degree.
> The upper case means major or dominant, and lower case means
> minor. Ultimately you just need to memorize the quality of chord
> for each scale tone.

**Significance (p. 27).** Lays out the foundational framework for reading chord numerals and qualities that every subsequent chord analysis in the book depends on.

## Minor Diatonic Series caveat

> Minor Diatonic Series of 7th Chords
> is different from the Major Diatonic Series in that it more so rep-
> resents common chord choices in the minor tonality than an actual
> harmonized scale. It is much more complicated to come up with
> a true scale to harmonize because there are the three different
> minor scales to consider. This becomes problematic when trying
> to provide only one set of roman numeral scale degrees. An entire
> chapter could be dedicated to this, but for now for simplicities sake,
> understand there are borrowed tones from the different scales, re-
> sulting in an imperfect harmonization

**Significance (p. 28).** Corrects a common theoretical misconception by flagging that the minor diatonic chart is a practical convention, not a strict harmonization, preventing misapplication of the framework.

## Relative keys definition

> Major and minor keys that share the same key signature are called rel-
> ative keys. Essentially, they share the exact same notes as each other.

**Significance (p. 32).** Defines relative keys, the central harmonic concept around which the entire Autumn Leaves analysis is built.

## Finding relative key trick

> From the minor key you can either memorize its relative key or use this
> trick: think a major key a minor 3% up from the minor key (or vise-versa).

**Significance (p. 32).** Gives a practical interval-based rule for locating any relative major from a minor key, making the concept immediately actionable on any instrument.

## Minor IV chord to dominant

> The IV chord in a minor key is usually minor,
> but jazz musicians are constantly turning minor chords into dominant 7
> chords. You'll see that happen a lot more throughout our studies!

**Significance (p. 33).** States a pervasive jazz harmonic practice — modal mixture of the minor IV to dominant — that recurs throughout the repertoire studied in the book.

## ii-V-I as cornerstone

> When it comes to jazz, there is no more important chord progression
> to know than the ii-V-l. You will see it come up time and time again,
> and so you need to be able to recognize it right away, and of course,
>
> THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK
>
>
>
> know how to improvise over it. The ii-V-| is the cornerstone chord pro-
> gression in jazz.

**Significance (p. 34).** Declares the ii-V-I the single most important chord progression in jazz, establishing the priority students should give it in recognition and improvisation.

## Guide tones definition

> Guide tones are notes within a chord structure that both help define a
> chord, and can be used to transition to another chord melodically.

**Significance (p. 36).** Defines guide tones precisely, introducing the primary improvisational tool taught in this chapter's improv lesson.

## Guide tones are 3rds and 7ths

> In jazz, the guide tones are almost always the 3rds and 7ths of each
> chord.

**Significance (p. 36).** Specifies exactly which chord tones function as guide tones in jazz, giving students a concrete and immediately applicable rule.

## 3rds and 7ths define chord quality

> The 3rds and 7ths define the chords in this progression. 7“ chords are
> built: Root-3'9-5%-7". The root is never altered, and the 5“ is never al-
> tered unless it is a half-diminished or fully diminished 7% chord. Even
> still, the 5% is not a strong note in the chord. But the 3% and 7* are
> pivotal in defining whether a chord is major, minor, dominant, half-di-
> minished, or fully diminished.

**Significance (p. 37).** Explains why 3rds and 7ths are the guide tones by showing they are the only chord members that change to differentiate chord quality, grounding the rule in music theory.

## Voice leading definition

> Voice leading is the smooth melodic movement of notes (or voices)
> from one chord to the next.

**Significance (p. 37).** Defines voice leading as a companion concept to guide tones, establishing the principle of stepwise resolution that makes guide-tone lines musical.

## 7th resolves to 3rd in ii-V-I

> Notice how the 7* of each chord resolves to the 3 of the next chord.
> It happens to work perfectly this way because the chords are cycling
> in 4ths. But the basic concept is the closest step-wise motion you can
> make from the 7‘ of a chord in this ii-V-l, is the 3 of the next. That’s
> voice leading.

**Significance (p. 37).** Describes the specific voice-leading mechanism — 7th resolving by step to the 3rd of the next chord — that students must internalize to connect guide tones into a melodic line.

## Guide tones practice method

> It's a great practice to play them isolated with either half notes or
> quarter notes through the entire form of a song, and so that is precise-
> ly what we are going to do.

**Significance (p. 38).** Prescribes the concrete practice method for internalizing guide tones — isolated half or quarter notes through the full form — giving students a repeatable exercise template.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
